faqs.question: Is soft tissue ultrasound painful?
faqs.answer: No. You may feel slight pressure from the probe, but no pain.

faqs.question: How long does a soft tissue ultrasound take?
Can I eat before the scan?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless your abdomen is also being examined—confirm with our booking desk.

faqs.question: Will I get the report on the same day?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hard copy and digital report available shortly after your scan at Cadabams Diagnostics.

faqs.question: Is it safe for children?
faqs.answer: Absolutely—no radiation, safe for all ages.

faqs.question: What if the scan detects an abnormality?
faqs.answer: Our radiologist will explain the next steps and, if needed, coordinate further tests or a specialist referral within the Cadabams network.

seo.title: Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan | Cadabams Diagnostics
seo.description: Painless Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan at Cadabams: Non-invasive, quick diagnosis of lumps/inflammation. No radiation. Prep guide & booking info included.

test.markdown:
## Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan Overview

### What Makes Soft Tissue Ultrasound a First-Line Imaging Choice
Soft tissue ultrasound scan is often recommended before any other imaging test.
• \*\*Real-time pictures\*\* help doctors watch muscles, tendons, and ligaments move as you move.
• \*\*No radiation\*\* makes it safe for children, pregnant women, and repeated follow-ups.
• \*\*High-resolution images\*\* of superficial structures such as skin lumps, joint swelling, and tendon tears.
### How Cadabams Diagnostics Ensures Accurate Results
• \*\*Latest high-frequency probes\*\* capture even sub-millimetre lesions.
• \*\*Certified sonographers\*\* follow strict protocols for positioning and scanning angles.
• \*\*Immediate review\*\* by on-site radiologists so you leave with reliable answers.
## What is Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan?
### Definition and Technology Behind the Scan
A \*\*soft tissue ultrasound scan\*\* uses high-frequency sound waves to create pictures of structures beneath the skin—muscles, tendons, ligaments, fat, and superficial blood vessels. A small handheld probe glides over a layer of gel on the skin; sound waves bounce back and are converted into live images on the screen.
### Difference Between B-Mode, Color-Doppler and Elastography
| Mode | What It Shows | When We Use It |
|---|---|---|
| \*\*B-Mode (2-D)\*\* | Greyscale anatomy | Baseline scan for size, shape, texture |
| \*\*Color-Doppler\*\* | Blood flow direction & speed | Rule out vascular malformations, assess inflammation |
| \*\*Elastography\*\* | Tissue stiffness | Differentiate benign vs malignant lumps |
## Types of Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan
### Superficial Soft Tissue Ultrasound
Targets skin, subcutaneous fat, and small nerves—ideal for lipomas, sebaceous cysts, or foreign bodies.
### Deep Soft Tissue and Musculoskeletal Ultrasound
Focuses on muscles, tendons, ligaments, and joint recesses—used for rotator-cuff tears, Achilles tendinopathy, or muscle hematomas.
### Ultrasound-Guided Procedures
Real-time needle guidance for:
• Cyst or abscess drainage
• Steroid or PRP injections
• Biopsy of suspicious lumps

## When and Who Needs to Take a Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan?

### Symptoms that Warrant the Scan
• A new or growing \*\*lump or bump\*\* under the skin
• \*\*Swelling\*\* after injury that does not settle in 48–72 hours
• \*\*Painful snapping\*\* in a tendon during movement
• \*\*Red, warm skin\*\* suggesting abscess or infection
• \*\*Reduced joint motion\*\* following sports or gym activity
### Age Groups Most Likely to Need the Test
• \*\*Children & Teens\*\*: sports injuries, congenital lumps
• \*\*Working Adults\*\*: repetitive strain injuries, desk-job related tendonitis
• \*\*Elderly\*\*: muscle tears, evaluation of lipomas or skin lesions

## Risks & Limitations of the Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

### Safety Profile and Known Risks
• \*\*Zero ionising radiation\*\* – completely safe.
• \*\*No known side effects\*\* when performed by trained staff.
• Very rare: minor skin irritation from gel.
### Limitations Compared to MRI or CT for Deep Tissues
• Sound waves cannot penetrate \*\*bone or air-filled bowel\*\*—deep pelvic or chest lesions may need MRI/CT.
• \*\*Operator-dependent\*\*—experience matters.
• \*\*Obesity\*\* can reduce image clarity for deeper structures.

## List of Parameters Considered During the Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

1. \*\*Echogenicity Patterns\*\* – dark (hypoechoic) vs bright (hyperechoic) areas hint at fluid, fat, or calcification.
2. \*\*Vascularity via Doppler\*\* – increased blood flow may indicate inflammation or tumour.
3. \*\*Size, Shape, and Margins of Lesions\*\* – round, smooth edges often benign; irregular, infiltrative margins need further tests.

## Reasons for Taking a Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

### Investigating Lumps or Swelling
Quickly determine whether a lump is solid or fluid-filled, and if it needs removal or observation.
### Evaluating Sports Injuries
Visualise muscle fibre tears, tendon thickness, or joint effusion within minutes of arrival.
### Monitoring Known Lesions
Track size changes in lipomas or post-injection hematomas without repeated radiation exposure.

## Benefits of Taking the Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

• \*\*Non-invasive and painless\*\* – just gel and gentle probe pressure.
• \*\*Real-time imaging\*\* – see structures move while you move.
• \*\*Cost-effective and quick turnaround\*\* – most scans complete in 15–30 minutes; reports ready the same day at Cadabams Diagnostics.

## Illnesses Diagnosed with Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

| Condition | How Ultrasound Helps |
|---|---|
| \*\*Lipomas & Sebaceous Cysts\*\* | Confirms fatty or cystic nature; guides removal. |
| \*\*Abscesses & Hematomas\*\* | Locates pus or blood collection; aids drainage. |
| \*\*Tendon and Ligament Tears\*\* | Shows fibre disruption, fluid gaps, or thickening. |
| \*\*Soft Tissue Tumors\*\* | Differentiates benign from suspicious masses; directs biopsy. |

## Preparing for Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan

### Clothing and Skin Preparation Tips
• Wear \*\*loose clothing\*\* that can be rolled up or removed easily.
• Remove \*\*jewellery\*\* near the area being examined.
• Keep skin \*\*clean and free of lotions or oils\*\*.
### Fasting or Medication Guidelines (If Any)
• \*\*No fasting\*\* required for superficial or limb scans.
• If abdomen or pelvis is also being examined, 4–6-hour fasting may be advised—clarify when booking.
## Pre-requisites Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan
• \*\*Doctor’s Referral\*\* – bring the printed or digital prescription.
• \*\*Previous Imaging Reports\*\* – MRI, X-ray, or earlier ultrasound for comparison.
## Best Time to Take the Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan
### Ideal Timing After Symptom Onset
• \*\*Acute injuries\*\*: within 1–7 days to assess bleeding or tears.
• \*\*Chronic lumps\*\*: any time; sooner if growth or pain increases.
### When to Avoid the Scan
• \*\*Open wounds or infected skin\*\* over the target area—wait until healed.
• \*\*Severe skin conditions\*\* that prevent probe contact.
## Eligibility
### Age and Health Conditions
• \*\*All age groups\*\*—infants to elderly.
• \*\*Safe in pregnancy\*\* for limb or superficial structures.
### Contraindications
• \*\*None absolute\*\*—only relative caution with fresh surgical scars or active skin infection.
## Procedure for Taking a Soft Tissue Ultrasound Scan
1. \*\*Check-in\*\* at reception with referral and ID.
2. \*\*Brief history\*\* taken by the sonographer—symptoms, past surgery.
3. \*\*Positioning\*\* on the exam couch; clothing adjusted to expose the area.
4. \*\*Gel application\*\* for sound conduction.
5. \*\*Scanning\*\* with gentle probe movements—10–20 minutes depending on area.
6. \*\*Wipe-off gel\*\*, get dressed, and proceed to report collection.
## Caution Before Taking the Test
• \*\*Inform technicians\*\* about \*\*pacemakers, implants\*\*, or \*\*prosthetic joints\*\* in the scan area.
• \*\*Cover open wounds\*\* with sterile dressing; scan may be postponed.
• \*\*Pregnancy\*\*: always mention gestational age; scans away from the uterus are safe.
